metadata.title: Decoding the star system: Twitter and its impact on journalism in the global South

abstract: Changes brought about by the digitalisation of journalism in the global South have implications for the role that the press plays in politics and governance, particularly in terms of giving a voice to those on the margins. This paper presents original evidence from India to consider how Twitter is reshaping the hierarchies within journalism and how it contributes to a 'star system' in Indian media institutions whereby national journalists as well as celebrities have gained more influence over journalists working in the regional press. Drawing on public profiles of over 50 journalists, while anonymizing individual identities, we computationally map their follower-following networks on Twitter and analyse the resulting structures to show how they intensify the historical divide between the Delhi-based legacy institutions and the vernacular language media. Besides using network graphs, we develop topic models of the biographies of journalists from different social cohorts and find a divergence in the way celebrity-seeking journalists use Twitter more effectively than those working in the regional press, with the latter not able to match the strategic use of the network for personal branding by the former cohort. We conclude that this divergence can also be contributing towards a star system like structure of journalism online with readership and influence flowing to those at the centre. We conclude with recommendations for editors, journalists as well as technology developers on how to avoid such outcomes.

paragraphs: The emergence of digital platforms like Twitter brought about a wave of euphoria among the optimists who saw the internet heralding a 'golden age' of journalism in India (Jeffrey, 2014). Overlooking the historical weakness in press autonomy (Khan, 2018) and their role as political institutions of governance (Cook, 2006) these accounts emphasized how the Indian media became early adopters of networking platforms. This generated a false sense of optimism that social media like Twitter will enable a more democratic public sphere that will automatically reform the institutional arrangements by enabling those on the margins to move to the mainstream. But two decades after the arrival of Web 2.0, and with near universal acceptance of platforms like Twitter as the default networking platforms for journalists, the initial optimism about the digital media's democratic impact has given way to more and more pessimism. Rather than making the field more equal, Twitter seems to have concentrated influence and attention of the viewers from the many to a select few.

It thus becomes important to empirically map the way journalists' network on platforms like Twitter, and consider how influence and reach online varies across different social cohorts. This paper presents an original study of journalists on Twitter in India, a country with the world's largest digital media user base with news reporters and editors among the most prolific users of the platform. It draws a comparison between Delhi-based journalists associated with the legacy and national media mainstream and journalists based in the more peripheral regions who face insularity for being at a distance from power and capital. In drawing a picture of journalism networks on Twitter, we argue that while the platform represents a broad cross-section of national and regional media institutions, online networking has not altered the preexisting hierarchies, which had divided the mainstream journalists from their regional peers in the past.

In fact, the paper argues, that the 'follower-following' model of Twitter has only exacerbated these divides-a trend that can also be seen in the distinct strategies adopted by celebrity journalists gaining more influence over those in the regional media institutions. In particular, we show that journalists from the regional press, particularly those who are in need for greater support and recognition for their work on ground have experienced a change in an opposite direction, whereby they have become cogs within the networks dominated by prominent electronic media journalists as well as legacy media and entertainment celebrities. In other words, the promise of a more equal public sphere has been replaced by a global but even more deeply hierarchical structure online. The networks formed among journalists serve those who already have more resources and better access to corporate power while journalists working in regional arenas gravitate towards celebrity and entertainment-based handles.

These issues aren't unique to the Indian press and even in global North, including the press in the United States of America, there is growing evidence that elite journalists tend to isolate from the rest of the press corps, locked in what Usher and Ng (2020) call 'elite bubbles.' Our 'star system' model is similar to this trend but in addition, it also captures the way in which celebrities influence regional journalists, while the latter remain cut-off from peers working in the other regional media. In this way the global South faces further marginalization with those on the margins remaining on the peripheries and only a few national figures occupy central positions. In the absence of horizontal structures linking regional journalists with one another, journalism Twitter in India resembles such a star system (with celebrities in the middle and all others directly bound to them). We argue that this structure can only weaken the possibility of forging solidarities among non-elite journalists since they are ever less likely to get out of the spheres of influence of entertainment and national celebrities.

In terms of methodology, we combine field-based research identifying different journalist cohorts in the Indian media and also use computational methods to show complementarity in our findings from both the approaches. Our analytical focus is on the clustering that occurs in network plots formed on the basis of networking practices of journalists on Twitter as well as by observing the variance in the biographies of journalists from the national and regional context using topic modelling. The study highlights an emergent gap between the national-level handles of celebrity journalists who tend to be more strategic in their Twitter use versus those working in the regional press.

In conclusion, we argue that rather than only seeing the Internet's globalising effects, media policy experts and journalism practitioners need to pay greater attention to the growing disconnect among the vernacular media and the highly unequal hierarchies in terms of the influence the national cohort gains online. The starsystem structures the way in which digital platforms like Twitter not only create elite bubbles but also reduce non-celebrity voices further to the margins where these are ever more unlikely to be heard by peers who are on Twitter but remain unaware of one another.

sections.paper_id: Khan et al. - 2023 - Decoding the star system Twitter and its impact on journalism in the global South
sections.header_raw: | T WIT TER AND JOURNALISM IN THE GLOBAL SOUTH: ELITE NET WORKS
sections.header_norm: t wit ter and journalism in the global south elite net works
paragraphs: The theoretical focus of the article draws from a debate in the literature about the value, relevance and implications of globalisation represented by the rise of digital platforms for democratising national public and media institutions. Journalism being a significant aspect of modern public sphere, particularly in the way it remains autonomous of the state, and a critical space for 'truthtelling' (Kovach & Rosenstiel, 2014), becomes an obvious place to start investigating the debate about the impact of technological globalisation worldwide. This is particularly relevant in the context of democratic states, where truth-seeking and fighting disinformation relies on public institutions (Elkins & Norris, 2012) and digital media are often seen as a means to empower those who have long been subordinated in the era of print and television media systems.

In the early days of the Internet, there was optimism that increasing number of journalists identifying and branding themselves on platforms like Twitter, could engender more direct and one-to-one interactions with readers, which could give way to the revival of small and non-legacy media institutions (Bruns, 2012). Across the world, Twitter emerged as a truly global platform where any individual journalist with a smart phone and internet connectivity could follow another, and could network directly with peers as well as with readers, making it one of a kind social media platform (Alejandro, 2010). However, it is not obvious that such networking would necessarily make the news culture more democratic in the global South and there's need for more clarity about the impact by considering empirical studies based on the experience of journalists in countries such as India.

Such concerns are particularly significant in the context of the global South because the resources available to journalists in advanced capitalist societies do not exist and state institutions are often stronger in comparison to the civil society (Zhao, 2012). Moreover, we find that even in democratic societies such as India, given an expansive digital divide, while fewer than 20% of all mobile phone users have access to social media platforms like Twitter, most journalists are still likely to rely on the platform for news sourcing and professional networking. As recent studies have shown, Twitter is increasingly a media used

paragraphs: DECODING THE STAR SYSTEM ON TWITTER for political mobilisation but its impact on garnering support for weaker groups tends be relatively weaker in the global South (Sinha et al., 2023).

Twitter as a networking platform seems to have a particular appeal among journalists in India given the fact that it is also a direct interface with many other media celebrities, including the most popular sports and other national and international entertainment personalities. In addition, in India a new crop of media-savvy national politicians also use Twitter extensively in election campaigns (Neyazi et al., 2016). While being on Twitter may give a sense of being in touch with famous personalities, and keeping up with national news, it is unclear if the platform has the potential for reducing the insularity of those working in the regional and vernacular press. In the global South, the insularity is further exacerbated due to persistence material inequality between those working outside the metropolitan areas, which also means a greater need for cooperation among those who face similar challenges. These linkages would be another instance of 'South-South' cooperation, where solidarity among the subaltern states and people is seen as significant in overcoming their historical and structural disadvantages compared to those located in the global North (Gray & Gills, 2015).

Even in the context of the global North, studies show that Twitter has become a platform which serves the 'media elites' (Lichter et al., 1986;Usher & Ng, 2020). So if we are to evaluate how Twitter could be a media where journalists lacking material and intellectual resources can build social capital, we need to be attentive to these historical inequalities, which exist among journalists and then analyse the existing social hierarchies that exist in the press as a public institution. Instead of simply conceptualising Indian journalism on Twitter as a homogenous field of action, we need to contrast the strategies adopted by those journalists working in the national media with greater resources, for instance journalists working in legacy print and Delhi based electronic media outlets, and those who work in regional contexts and have to deal with constant resource constrains.

Besides journalists' networks, we also need to consider the extent to which each of these social cohorts use Twitter strategically towards 'celebritisation' or 'celebrification' (Driessens, 2013) and the way these trends shape the influence they have over their peers and followers online. It is only within such a conceptual focus on understanding the divide between the national and regional cohorts of journalists that we can study the way in which journalists from different social cohorts adopt distinct networking strategies on Twitter. Moreover, considering that the platform identifies as a 'micro-blogging' site, we also need to pay attention to the self-presentation by journalists on the platform (Olausson, 2017).

In our study, which aims to draw on such a theoretical framework, we aim to develop an analytical mapping of not only the networks among journalists from distinct cohorts but also contrast the biographies used by journalists from non-elite and regional backgrounds on Twitter. The focus of our framework is thus on two related theoretical puzzles -the first concerns the insularity of different social cohorts online and the second concerns the rise of the celebrity-style strategic use of networking. Before we begin to analyse these trends in India, it is important to discuss some of the distinct historical and institutional features of journalism in the country in more detail and bring the focus on the distinctions across different social groups, which is relevant for identifying the most significant cohorts. With this approach we can then begin to operationalise our conceptual framework in the field.

sections.header_raw: | JOURNALISM AND REG IONAL PRESS IN IND IA: AN INSTITUTION ALRE ADY IN PERIL
sections.header_norm: journalism and reg ional press in ind ia an institution alre ady in peril
paragraphs: Before we start to analyse the impact of Twitter on journalism it is important to recognise that regional institutions are very important in the global South, for instance in India, which was already a highly unequal society long before social media emerged in the late 2000s (Kohli, 2006). In particular, growing inequalities produced by a centralized model of development often mean that regional institutions were on the margins compared to the national mainstream, with Delhi-based news media enjoying greater access to the political and economic elites, as well as possessing greater means and economic resources. It might look like there is a phenomenon similar to the more developed countries, for instance the 'Beltway elite' in the USA, in India the politics of media system is further undermined by structural inequality within and across its regions and a lack of genuine 'autonomy' in spite of multiple reforms since the end of the colonial rule (Khan, 2018).

Until the late 20th century, much of the media institutions in the global South and particularly the communications infrastructure remained in the grip of colonial powers (Boyd-Barrett, 2014), and even after economic liberalisation of the 1990s, most of the nonmetropolitan, so called 'tier 2' regions, did not receive adequate support, with benefits accruing disproportionately to Delhi-based media and political entities. But the problem of autonomy existed across the board, with both the national and regional media facing resource problems that forced them into self-censorship. Perhaps the situation is more evident in the region known as the 'Hindi heartland'-the northern Indian states such as Uttar Pradesh, which remain at the bottom of human development within the country. While politics extracts a cruel toll on the day today life of journalists in the state, access to technology as well as readership in the heartland also falls short when compared to media markets in the economically richer states and other regions of the country (Ninan, 2007).

Hence, even more than in the case of the global North, digital media and online networking opens the possibility of disruption and weakening the grip of legacy media over the governance system. Moreover, with growing number of citizens in India moving towards smart phones and video-based content, it is possible for regional journalists to post and share news directly to audiences via platforms like YouTube. 1 Even before the rise of the digital media, there was an already existing divide between the colonial and post-independence era legacy print and television media institutions. The former had always been more devoted to higher quality reporting in English rather than higher quantities of news which became a forte of Hindi television news channels. The latter were more successful in becoming commercially viable by producing more output-with journalists in the Hindi national newspapers and television media gaining celebrity status more than any print media journalist could imagine in the era before television.

However, since the rise of the Hindu nationalist regime in 2014, a section of the electronic news media sector has become ever more reliant on sensational coverage rather than ground reporting except for a small but highly influential sections of the English language legacy press. As scholars focussing on the trajectory of economic liberalisation have argued, in India, a genre of 'entertaining news', accessed by most through television, has become a privileged domain of politics (Chakravartty & Roy, 2013). Even as most Hindi electronic news media have become more entertainment driven channels, one can still find journalists in the English-and Hindi language legacy outlets who possess a much higher degree of individual recognition for their serious reporting style and focus on major news events.

Today, there are individual journalists within the legacy press who have retained wide-spread recognition and even celebrity status online for precisely these aspects, while many Hindi media anchors and television personalities are seen as 'faces' of their respective channels and are rarely accorded the recognition given to the select individual journalists seen to be doing more serious public service style reporting and analysis. Many of the entertainment-heavy channels are based in the Delhi suburb of NOIDA and these often compete with one another in reporting sensational news events. In terms of their political economy, while the English language legacy press have increasingly oriented towards the global markets, the Hindi electronic media caters to the mass audiences in the Hindi heartland and tends to gravitate towards an editorial line that aligns with the ruling regime in Delhi.

It is thus important to distinguish between the two social cohorts within the elite sections of national press, and explore how each group networks on platforms like Twitter, which has become the most popular platform among journalists over the last ten years. 2 In contrast to these two sections, journalists in the regional press, particularly those working in vernacular language media outlets, lack the support both from Delhi's political institutions as well as the global markets and thus have to rely entirely on their audiences and readers who usually lack the monetary heft even if they can access global platforms such as Twitter. Hence, it is quite likely that this cohort of journalists, usually referred to as 'kshetriya patrakar' (regional reporter), would want to network with their peers in other regional language media, but historically, the absence of a shared platform left them with little access to readership and revenues outside the heartland.

It is fair to hypothesise that global networking platforms like Twitter can break these borders and also break the existing hierarchies, and favour more 'vernacularisation' and a simultaneous resurgence in subaltern activism which has been noticed in the context of electoral mobilizations in the recent years (Michelutti, 2007;Ninan, 2007). Hence, any study that aims to understand the impact of Twitter in the global South, must pay attention to such possibilities while keeping an eye on the persistence of historical hierarchies among journalists from different social cohorts. It need not only focus on class division or institutional divides between English language legacy print and commercially oriented Hindi electronic news media but also on the multi dimensional distinctions discussed so far.

On the flip-side, one has to be mindful of the fact that regional journalists would also be more prone to being drawn into the sphere of celebrities as an escape from the highly polarised and often vulnerable conditions, which define their everyday experiences. 3 Hence, while conceptualising the distinct social cohorts among journalists, in our study we differentiate between individual journalists in legacy media institutions and the commercially successful electronic press in NOIDA and also contrast both these cohorts from regional journalists who are embedded in their distinct vernacular milieu. In short, instead of simply selecting one language press or a region, we chose an institutional approach to study the impact of Twitter on the press. Our analysis remains attentive to such internal diversities while selecting the key cohorts and then developing an analytical account of the contrast in the use of Twitter across these cohorts.
Reflecting on the discussion so far, we can now frame the questions to be probed in following terms:

1. How are the social networking practices of journalists on Twitter different across the three cohorts, particularly between journalists working in legacy press institutions who have gained celebrity like status and those working in the NOIDA based electronic media who do not have a celebrity style public recognition as yet?
DECODING THE STAR SYSTEM ON TWITTER

• A secondary question concerns the way in which strategies of regional journalists differ from these two groups, since they not only lack individual celebrity but also the in-person networks of the other two groups.

sections.paper_id: Khan et al. - 2023 - Decoding the star system Twitter and its impact on journalism in the global South
paragraphs: Our research design and computational methodology draws from the theoretical framework and the questions discussed above. In this section we have shared details of how computational analysis helps us resolve the core conceptual themes. Here, we also draw from the previous study of journalists who are working outside of the mainstream media and their social media practice (Khan et al., 2019). Similar work in the context of use of social media has also revealed that those with most active presence online can sometimes be less involved in ground-level reporting. Hence, the best possible way to operationalise our framework was to remain mindful of the fact that digital presence can often be deceptive and hence instead of beginning by simply scraping user profile data of celebrity journalists from Twitter, we began our study with immersive field research and working directly with journalists to draw a small but purposive sample of journalists on the basis of the categories developed in the earlier section. This work was undertaken in the course of the pandemic so most of our interactions were through phone and online discussion, which began in late 2021, and continued through the year 2022 and progressed smoothly once the COVID-19 related restrictions were lifted in the middle of that year.

Once the first round was complete, two authors separately also undertook field trips to major regional centres in Western UP, to tier two towns including Aligarh and Bareilly, which have a sizable presence of vernacular papers. This helped in identifying local journalists who are not very well known outside of the region. Additionally, we cross-checked and ensured that all regional journalists also had an active presence on Twitter and we had to leave out those who did not. Through such purposive selection methods, we were able to operationalise the conceptual framework and simultaneously began our analysis in the first half of the year 2022 while also carrying out computational work at the same time.

In the end, our analysis relied upon a list of 50 names which we believe constituted a decent sample size to begin our exploratory research with the expectation that in due course and with more resources and access we will develop this research further. In order to ensure clarity, we put these journalists into three simple pools, defining the same as (1) national media journalists with celebrity status (2) national-level journalists mostly working in electronic news media but yet to have celebrity status and finally (3) regional journalists who obviously lack national celebrity status.

While selecting the regional cohort, the number came to N = 22 journalists and they represented five vernacular languages and 10 different regional news outlets. The sample includes journalists that have active Twitter accounts and can serve the inferential goals of the study. For the national non-celebrity cohort, we deployed a slightly different approach, and worked with a journalist from the national media, Author 2 (A2) relied extensively on a snowball and purposive sampling technique to create a list of N = 21 journalists working in over 10 different organisations who also have an active Twitter presence.

For the purpose of maintaining consistency and considering the objective of the study is to identify gaps in Twitter usage of different social cohorts of Indian journalists, the authors have used smaller but purposive samples that can provide necessary conclusions for further replication of the study. Finally, for the cohort of national journalists with a celebrity presence, we relied on mostly the Delhi-based group of journalists associated with the prestige press (all of them either in English or Hindi) who also enjoyed high number of followers on Twitter (in the range of >100,000 followers). The high number reflected their much-oversized reach on Twitter, which was many times more than that of the non-celebrity journalists whose average ranged to mostly <10,000 followers. 4 In total, a list of 53 journalists was prepared and classified into three categories: non-celebrity regional, noncelebrity national, and celebrity national journalists. After months of field research, we then conducted computational analysis of the 53 Twitter handles, with most of the data collected from Twitter APIs between February 2022 and March 2022. For the purpose of maintaining ethics in analysing the data, individual journalist handles were anonymised and only their general social cohort was referred in the analysis below. For the purpose of indication, we have added list of select representative media institutions for each cohort, in the Appendix S1.

For the national non-celebrity cohort, we deployed a slightly different approach, and worked with a journalist from the national media, Author 2 (A2) relied extensively on a snowball and purposive sampling technique to create a list of N = 21 journalists working in over 10 different organisations who also have an active Twitter presence.

For the purpose of maintaining consistency and considering the objective of the study is to identify gaps in Twitter usage of different social cohorts of Indian journalists, the authors have used smaller but purposive samples that can provide necessary conclusions for further replication of the study. Finally, for the cohort of national journalists with a celebrity presence, we relied on mostly the Delhi-based group of journalists associated with the prestige press (all of them either in English or Hindi) who also enjoyed high number of followers on Twitter (in the range of >100,000 followers). The high number reflected their much-oversized reach on Twitter, which was many times more than that of the non-celebrity journalists whose average ranged to mostly <10,000 followers. 4 In total, a list of 53 journalists was prepared and classified into three categories: non-celebrity regional, noncelebrity national, and celebrity national journalists. After months of field research, we then conducted computational analysis of the 53 Twitter handles, with most of the data collected from Twitter APIs between February 2022 and March 2022. For the purpose of maintaining ethics in analysing the data, individual journalist handles were anonymised and only their general social cohort was referred in the analysis below. For the purpose of indication, we have added list of select representative media institutions for each cohort, in the Appendix S1.

paragraphs: The same appendix also gives figures for the Twitter follower base of each cohort and it is easy to see a clear distinction between those we labelled celebrity versus those who are not so. This can be observed in each of the following figures, where the national celebrity journalists have the highest followers (on avg. between 100,000 ≤ 1 million) while regional journalists have the least, and in terms of the following the order is just the opposite.

As is obvious from the graphs above, while celebrity journalists in the legacy press clearly have a vast lead in terms of the followers, it is the cohort of non-celebrity regional journalists, which is likely to follow back the largest number of handles on Twitter.

Hence, in order to better understand the strategies adopted by the different cohorts of journalists, we employed computational social network analysis. For the network analysis of each cohort, a systematic sampling method was used on the obtained stratified sample of journalists. We prepared the network graphs that were based on the handles the journalists in each cohort followed. We extracted the ten most recent followings and the ten earliest followings of each journalist from each cohort to prepare a network. This process was applied for every journalist in all the three social cohorts. The list of individual handles followed by individual journalists within each cohort was combined and then used to develop a network graph that was representative of the network strategy of each cohort.

Based on this method, the final sample size of each cohort after obtaining the 'following' of each journalist and after eliminating duplication, the number of nodes (N) for each of the three graphs were as following: Noncelebrity regional journalists: 431, Non-celebrity national journalists: 423 and for the Celebrity National journalists it was 194. The total number of connections observed between the nodes after eliminating all the duplicates were as follows, (1) Non-celebrity regional journalists: 8736 connections, (2) Non-celebrity national journalists: 9309 and (3) Celebrity National journalists: 3809.

These nodes and connections were then plugged into Gephi to visualise the network graphs and the nature of the graphs was then analysed. Particularly in terms of identifying the distinct groups that exist within the overall graph, we computed modularity for each network graph to distinguish the sub-communities. Calculating modularity enabled us to detect clusters and resulted in partitioning clusters having high intracluster density. Force Atlas graph layout was applied to the network graph, enabling a structure where most connected nodes are pushed to the periphery of the network and nodes connected with them are aligned around them. The sizing of the nodes is done on the basis of in-degree centrality.

paragraphs: Besides the network data, we also collected data from the biographies of the followings of the 53 journalists in the three cohorts. The biographies are publicly available on their landing pages and the same were extracted and compiled into a dataset. Data collection was done using the 'tweepy' module in Python 3, which is a wrapper for the Twitter API. We also extracted biographies of all following of the journalists and the first 100 followers, together it provided us a unique dataset that could be used to identify the kind of topics that emerge in the Twitter networks journalists of different cohorts. In this paper, we have shared topic models generated using biographies of the accounts journalists in each cohort follow. Details regarding the exact dates and method of extracting the biographies are also provided in Appendix S2.

paragraphs: Since understanding how journalists from the national and regional media have reorganised themselves as a community amid digitalisation forms the basis of our research, we used online social network analysis of the followings of the journalists in each cohort and identified clusters in each social network graph. In the network graphs, the nodes represent the community members, in this case, the journalists. In our analysis, we find that there is a degree of clustering observed in each cohort and as part of our findings, we first present the clustering observed in each of the three cohorts and also name them based on the most common features of the group.

sections.header_raw: | Celebrity journalists within legacy national media outlets
sections.header_norm: celebrity journalists within legacy national media outlets
paragraphs: As we can see in the Figure 1 above, which is based on the modularity measure within the network graph of the celebrity national journalist cohort, there are five clusters within it. The biggest cluster (marked in purple) is predominantly composed of journalists from the digital news media, which suggests a strong sense of community seems evident among them and indicates a growing visibility through their presence on platforms like Twitter where they are now firmly embedded with celebrity national media elites. The reason we identify this cluster in terms of 'digital media' is that the most common aspect in this group is that they come from digital-first news organisations such as Scroll, The Wire and Newslaundry which are not very commercially successful but have nonetheless established themselves as credible voices in the public sphere. The cluster also had individuals associated with some well recognised digital fact-checking news portals including BoomLive and AltNews.

This group emerged distinct (based on the modularity measure applied to the entire cohort) from the other cluster of journalists (marked in blue), which is just as elite but is primarily constituted of journalists a majority of whom are based in legacy print media. These two clusters, as can be seen, appear to be closely tied together, but they do emerge as distinct from one another. Again, the reason to identify the blue cluster in the terms of 'legacy and print' media is that the biggest and most central nodes in this cluster tend to be journalists from legacy broadcast and print media such as the The Hindu and NDTV, and reflect a distinct subcommunity of journalists many of whom can be argued to be the most visible face of some of the most powerful legacy media houses in Delhi.

The third, somewhat less prominent but nonetheless distinct cluster identified through our modularity analysis of the network of national celebrity journalists and their followings can be defined as 'Indian rightwing' (marked in green). As Appendix S1 clarifies, this cluster includes some of the most prominent political leaders of the Hindu nationalist party in power, the BJP, as well as official handles of current government ministries, which are playing a major role in national politics. Besides politicians, in this cluster there is a clear presence of right-leaning news channel personalities and journalists as well and some of these are listed in the Appendix S1 (in Section 3). This indicates that Hindu nationalism has managed to carve a significant presence and influence over elite legacy media.

Interestingly, in the celebrity national journalist cohort graph, we find only a few high-value industrialists and non-political celebrities, and they are followed mostly by journalists covering beats such as economics and finance. The absence of Bollywood and cricket personalities suggests that the celebrity journalists in legacy national news media primarily follow those in power on Twitter and not necessarily other celebrities who may be equally influential but lack access to power, particularly in Delhi. This will be a theme to contrast this with the other two cohorts, where as we shall see, entertainment and sports personalities are much more widely followed.

To further support our analysis, we also computationally built topic models based on the biographies of the accounts journalists in this cohort follow. As the following Figures 2 and 3 show certain peculiar topic appear to dominate the biographies of the accounts journalists in this cohort follow on Twitter.

If we follow the 30 most commonly occurring words (in the Topic 4), as done in the above Figure 2 we can clearly see that the self-centred orientation of the cohort is the most common aspect of the biographies of their followings on Twitter. We can see that the most occurring terms are 'india', 'journalist', 'editor' and 'news', which suggests that followings of journalists in this cohort are more inclined towards issues of national level and often identify themselves as journalists in their biographies.

Similarly in the Figure 3, which plots the keywords appearing in Topic 9 (which appears very distinctly in the model) we find words like 'news' and 'India' appearing at the top, and it is the complete absence of a non-national figures or references to any other professional cohort (such as entertainment and cricket) that sets this group apart as perhaps the most strategic in their self-definition and least ambivalent in the purpose of its use of Twitter.

paragraphs: In contrast, when we see that clustering patterns that emerged when we computationally applied modularity measures on the social network graph of followings of 'Non-Celebrity National Journalists', we find a very different story. The pattern that emerges is shown in the Figure 4 below.

The biggest cluster (covering around a third of all nodes), and marked in purple in Figure 4, is one that centres around personalities from the field of films, entertainment and sports world in India. These handles were almost entirely missing in the previous cohort's network graph. Additionally, in this cohort we also observed that these entertainment figures are not necessarily the most powerful or distinguished personalities from respective domains, and perhaps the main reason they are being followed by this cohort of journalists is not because they see them as newsworthy but from a personal liking towards them. This is similar to the phenomenon also identified with the general tendency among the youth in urban India, popularly known as 'timepass'. This finding was further corroborated by the topic models (shown in Figure 5) built on the basis of the biographies of the followings of non-celebrity national journalists on Twitter. Here we find that words like 'actor', 'cricket' and 'film' appeared among the top 30 keywords used in self-descriptions in several topics. This was in contrast with the previous cohort, alongside the reoccurrence of names of several government ministries and political leaders from the ruling party within this cluster. In the topic models for this cohort, we find keywords like 'Offici', 'account', 'govern' and 'ministri' (as evidence in the Figure 6 below) appear quite often and reveals their interest in following official government accounts perhaps even more than national celebrity journalists.

Another cluster (indicated in blue in the Figure 4), can be identified as constituted of international media and consists of major foreign news and wire services, a select list of which is included in Appendix S1. This reflects interest of the middle-ranking journalists towards international wire services for latest updates, something that was missing among the previous cohort. In the second cohort's network graph, we also identify another cluster as Delhi-based English news media (indicated in Green in the Figure 4), which shows that many non-celebrity journalists tend to follow Delhi-based national news media even though few of them tend to be followed back by the latter.

The other cluster (marked in orange), that we found through modularity check, is constituted of more serious public personalities who are often cited for their research evidence and research in their work. Perhaps reflecting the work of 'beat reporters' following expert authorities on their beats such as the economy, this cluster primarily included economists, columnists, book authors and data journalists and was quite significant to be marked out distinct from others. Besides this, we also found a smaller South Indian regional cluster along the fringes of the network graph. It hardly included non-English media journalists, reflecting the bias in the national non-celebrity cohort to follow mostly English media journalists rather than their peers working in other regional languages.

One of the key features of this cohort is that they tend to have a greater reliance on entertainment as well as government institutions with few references to their own personal brands. The topic models based on their biographies and those handles that these journalists follow on Twitter, also reiterate this pattern.

As the Figure 5 above shows, when we modelled the words from the cohort, we find that words such as 'actor', and 'cricket' appear recurrently alongside words like 'ministr' and 'official'. sections.paper_id: Khan et al. - 2023 - Decoding the star system Twitter and its impact on journalism in the global South
paragraphs: Finally, we discuss the cohort of most interest to our paper, that of regional journalists who lack any celebrity status and only have a relatively low number of followers on Twitter. As can be seen from the Figure 7 below, once again like their national non-celebrity peers the overall cohort also splits into five clusters when we apply a modularity measure to the network graph formed on the basis of their Twitter network.

What is interesting is that this cohort had the highest score (modularity = 0.189) among the three cohorts, which indicates that they tend to have more dispersed clustering unlike the tightly knit cohort of celebrity national journalists who were almost like a well-knit community on Twitter. In the case of the regional journalists, the cohort seem to be split along language divide and each seem to have its own orientation towards multiple domains, including towards groups that one would not assume would be of interest to the regional press.

Consider for instance the most central cluster (in pink) which we labelled 'cricket and entertainment', since it had many handles from the current Indian Premier League (India's commercially successful cricket league, which had several regional franchises competing) along with many cinema and television media personalities. Moreover, given their regional orientation, while Hindi journalists cluster around Bollywood personalities, the other language journalists tend to follow celebrities from their own region, for instance journalists in the southern launguage news media follow several South Indian film personalities as well as well-known travel bloggers, celebrities from the southern Indian regional music industry.

The strong entertainment purpose of Twitter use in this cohort is also reflected in the topic models for the cohort's followings' biographies, where terms like 'music ','sports','entertain','cricket' and 'lifestyl' appear prominently (Figure 8 below).

Returning to the network graph of the cohort, the largest cluster in this cohort is the one we call 'Indian right-wing' (marked in blue in Figure 7), since handles of leading Hindu nationalist politicians seem to have a lot many more followers among the regional cohort, indicating a growing appeal of the ideology particularly among the journalists in the Hindi language regional press. Although we did not track how many of the leaders of the Hindu right-wing follow back these journalists, nonetheless, this clustering was a significant difference from the ideologically insular image that emerges from the network graphs of celebrity journalists in the national media.

However, lest we conclude in haste, in the case of regional journalists, another distinct cluster (marked in orange) is just as big in numbers and contains primarily handles of journalists from the Delhi-based legacy media houses. This cluster includes many of the handles of leaders of non-Hindutva parties. The distinctiveness of the blue and pink clusters indicates that regional media tends to follow all the camps closely, the Hindu nationalists as well as the handles of major oposition figures.

A final key observation about this cohort emerges when we unpack the group of topics that emerged distinct in the topic model graph (shown in Figure 9 F I G U R E 8 Topic Model created using biographies of Followings of 'Non-Celebrity Regional Journalists'.
F I G U R E 9 Topic Model created using biographies of Followings of 'Non-Celebrity Regional Journalists'.

find that besides entertainment, regional journalists also tend to orient themselves a lot more towards market and entrepreneurship. As we can see in the Topic Model in Figure 9 above, it shows that words such as 'founder' and 'profession' tend to be common in the biographies of the regional journalists and the handles they follow on Twitter.

sections.paper_id: Khan et al. - 2023 - Decoding the star system Twitter and its impact on journalism in the global South
paragraphs: Based on the above findings of the networks among the different cohorts of journalists as well as their self-styling on Twitter, we can draw some important inferences from it. We can observe clearly that 'regional non-celebrity' cohort is not any more insular given that they follow a range of national and even international personalities on Twitter. But seen in the context of our theoretical framework that highlights material and intellectual inequalities, another way to interpret it is to say that the vernacular media is also the most disoriented while the national celebrity media is the most strategic in the way they use Twitter to network with their peers.

This key observation compliments the basic metrics we observed earlier in the article, where we found that the celebrity group had the highest ratio calculated in terms of the number of followers they get for per handle they follow. In contrast, the regional cohort followed highest number of people on average while having the least number of followers. What perhaps is even more striking is the high degree of clustering within the latter cohort, which potentially does not allow them to discover or follow other regional journalists.

This little noticed aspect is perhaps an important negative effect of Twitter as a networking platform for journalists in the global South. It tends to reproduce the centralising features of legacy media and undermines, if not harmed, the potential for solidarity among regional journalists who clearly share more in common with their peers working in other vernacular media outlets. This outcome is a direct result of the recommendation system of the platform and the way it is built to serve and even promote those already with most followers. The platform has neither enabled greater interaction among regional journalists nor helped regional journalists discover their peers across the language divide. Instead, the recommendation system seems to have kept them hooked to national media and entertainment handles. This is a point of concern, since the regional journalists we selected for the study were not the novices on the platforms, and had reasonable degree of activity online. Many were well versed in English and some predominantly based in large regional capital cities such as Bengaluru and Chennai, so we had imagined that as a cohort they would be able to take advantage of the new media and perhaps be less oriented towards entertainment and political celebrities than had been the case of broadcast journalism. As Chakravartty and Roy (2013) showed the predominance of 'entertainment news' style was already a major factor in the vernacular media development and Twitter seems to exacerbate this trend further.

Moreover, we can see that the cohort of regional journalists that we selected was least strategic in using Twitter as compared to elite, national legacy journalists who already enjoy a celebrity status and lead others many times over in terms of the followers they have on Twitter. In this way, we can argue that Delhi-based legacy media journalists tend to use Twitter to further enhance their personal brands online. Even if we leave aside the elites, Twitter still does not seem to be an equal opportunity space and this becomes evident when we compare graphs between the non-celebrity national and regional cohorts.

Given their historical disadvantage, the regional media journalists have more reasons to be strategic online, and ideally this would mean building solidaritybased networks and perhaps relying on the Twitter to expand their readership base. But we found no such evidence of such 'South-South' collaboration. Perhaps the only positive signal was the distinct networking style of South India-based regional cohort, where we found that rather than orienting themselves towards Delhi, many journalists now follow their own regional cultural and film personalities. But again, even this cluster did not include any major non-English and non-Hindi handles, indicating that Southern journalism on Twitter has its own star system.

Overall, the major finding of interest from the institutional perspective is that the social networks on Twitter and topic models reflect a continuation of unequal power distribution in favour of the Delhi-based national celebrities and has perhaps further marginalised the regional voices while turning the Hindi language journalists into cogs in the celebrity driven star system. This kind of media elitism has also been observed by Usher and Ng (2020) who call out the Washingtonbased journalists for their insularity. With social media becoming important for journalists in the global South, a new media elitism seems to have arrived in India and celebrity culture has only deepened its roots via the digital space.

Instead of democratization, we can see a growing trend towards 'celebrification' (Driessens, 2013) which the platform has only intensified with policies such as paid 'blue-tick' verification. This will further enable celebrity influencers with greater access to resources to rope in regional journalists into their star system. In conclusion, we make the following policy recommendation, which can be relevant for the global governance of platforms like Twitter and particularly for vernacular journalists' associations looking to respond to global trends in social media development and prepare their professional communities to deal with its impact.

• With a clear distinction visible in networking practices adopted by journalists of varying socio-regionallinguistic backgrounds, encouraging greater network literacy and an awareness of algorithmic bias among journalists is of critical importance. • At the organisational and governance level, editors and senior journalists could initiate discussions about how online social media platforms such as Twitter have certain limitations in the way they make recommendations to their users. This would be a first step towards remedying the inequalities we encounter particularly in the regional institutions of the global South, because in countries such as India, these media institutions already struggle not only with resource crunch but also with structural inequality. • In addition, forging new 'South-South' inspired collaboration across different vernacular language media may also be a way to bring together audiences across language, with new AI based tools being used to overcome the existing divides. • Hence, we argue that naïve assumption that social media would on its own help build broader audience or visibility online will only lead to a further erosion of vernacular media markets. Hence, journalists could be encouraged to be more mindful of the existing hierarchies and historical privileges in their use of Twitter. At no point should follower count become a factor in their renumeration. • Instead, editors could encourage journalists to work more collaboratively and news media outlets can choose to develop joint multi-lingual digital media strategies. Perhaps even build partnerships with liberal and legacy media outlets, which often remain commercially less successful in regional news markets. • Finally, at the technology policy and global governance level, a push for providing a more transparent verification model should be made essential. • We would go as far as to suggest that platforms should do away with the 'follower-following' model and if they cannot then journalists could start working with developers to build alternative sites, including those which sponsor greater interaction among journalists working in vernacular language press and media institutions. • At the algorithmic level, networks that prioritise news and #hashtags be preferred rather than those whose recommendation system is opaque. But given the recent changes in its ownership, we believe Twitter is no longer fit as a model platform for networking for journalists, and as out study shows the promise of networking that emerged in the early 2010s has now been replaced by a star system.

Overall, the major finding of interest from the institutional perspective is that the social networks on Twitter and topic models reflect a continuation of unequal power distribution in favour of the Delhi-based national celebrities and has perhaps further marginalised the regional voices while turning the Hindi language journalists into cogs in the celebrity driven star system. This kind of media elitism has also been observed by Usher and Ng (2020) who call out the Washingtonbased journalists for their insularity. With social media becoming important for journalists in the global South, a new media elitism seems to have arrived in India and celebrity culture has only deepened its roots via the digital space.

Instead of democratization, we can see a growing trend towards 'celebrification' (Driessens, 2013) which the platform has only intensified with policies such as paid 'blue-tick' verification. This will further enable celebrity influencers with greater access to resources to rope in regional journalists into their star system. In conclusion, we make the following policy recommendation, which can be relevant for the global governance of platforms like Twitter and particularly for vernacular journalists' associations looking to respond to global trends in social media development and prepare their professional communities to deal with its impact.

• With a clear distinction visible in networking practices adopted by journalists of varying socio-regionallinguistic backgrounds, encouraging greater network literacy and an awareness of algorithmic bias among journalists is of critical importance. • At the organisational and governance level, editors and senior journalists could initiate discussions about how online social media platforms such as Twitter have certain limitations in the way they make recommendations to their users. This would be a first step towards remedying the inequalities we encounter particularly in the regional institutions of the global South, because in countries such as India, these media institutions already struggle not only with resource crunch but also with structural inequality. • In addition, forging new 'South-South' inspired collaboration across different vernacular language media may also be a way to bring together audiences across language, with new AI based tools being used to overcome the existing divides. • Hence, we argue that naïve assumption that social media would on its own help build broader audience or visibility online will only lead to a further erosion of vernacular media markets. Hence, journalists could be encouraged to be more mindful of the existing hierarchies and historical privileges in their use of Twitter. At no point should follower count become a factor in their renumeration. • Instead, editors could encourage journalists to work more collaboratively and news media outlets can choose to develop joint multi-lingual digital media strategies. Perhaps even build partnerships with liberal and legacy media outlets, which often remain commercially less successful in regional news markets. • Finally, at the technology policy and global governance level, a push for providing a more transparent verification model should be made essential. • We would go as far as to suggest that platforms should do away with the 'follower-following' model and if they cannot then journalists could start working with developers to build alternative sites, including those which sponsor greater interaction among journalists working in vernacular language press and media institutions. • At the algorithmic level, networks that prioritise news and #hashtags be preferred rather than those whose recommendation system is opaque. But given the recent changes in its ownership, we believe Twitter is no longer fit as a model platform for networking for journalists, and as out study shows the promise of networking that emerged in the early 2010s has now been replaced by a star system.

We can observe clearly that 'regional non-celebrity' cohort is not any more insular given that they follow a range of national and even international personalities on Twitter. But seen in the context of our theoretical framework that highlights material and intellectual inequalities, another way to interpret it is to say that the vernacular media is also the most disoriented while the national celebrity media is the most strategic in the way they use Twitter to network with their peers.
